enrichment.seo_title: Exim Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Exim is a free, GPL-licensed mail transfer agent powering cPanel hosting and Debian servers. See how it compares to Postfix and Sendmail in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Exim is a free and open-source mail transfer agent (MTA) - the background software responsible for routing, filtering, and delivering email between servers on Unix-like operating systems. Rather than a commercial SaaS product, Exim is infrastructure-level software: it listens on SMTP ports, accepts inbound mail, applies configurable access control and content-scanning rules, and routes outbound messages to their destinations. It is most commonly encountered by developers and system administrators indirectly, as the engine quietly handling mail behind shared-hosting control panels such as cPanel/WHM, and as the default MTA shipped with Debian Linux and many of its derivatives.

The project began in 1995 at the University of Cambridge Computing Service, where Philip Hazel wrote the first version as a successor to the older Smail-3 mailer, aiming for greater routing flexibility and stronger checks on incoming mail. The name Exim originally stood for 'EXperimental Internet Mailer,' reflecting its origins as an internal replacement project rather than a commercial product. In 1997, Hazel also authored the PCRE (Perl Compatible Regular Expressions) library specifically to give Exim's configuration language powerful pattern-matching, and PCRE went on to become one of the most widely embedded regex engines in other software, including PHP and older versions of Apache. Exim itself moved from the original PCRE library to PCRE2 in 2021 as the ecosystem modernized. Hazel remained the primary author and maintainer until his retirement from Cambridge at the end of September 2007, at which point stewardship passed to a small, unpaid group known informally as the Exim Maintainers, whose historical and current contributors include Heiko Schlittermann, Jeremy Harris, Nigel Metheringham, Tony Finch, Phil Pennock, and several others who coordinate development on GitHub and via the exim-dev and exim-users mailing lists. Because the project has no corporate backer or venture funding, practical matters such as domain registration are nominally held by an individual maintainer (Heiko Schlittermann) rather than a legal entity, and no maintainer is paid specifically for public Exim support.

Technically, Exim follows the classic Sendmail-style design of a single configurable binary that performs all MTA duties, moving through well-defined stages during which it deliberately gains or drops process privileges for security. Its configuration is organized into distinct sections - access control lists (ACLs) that decide whether to accept or reject a message at each SMTP stage, routers that decide how an address should be handled, transports that define how a message is actually delivered (local delivery, SMTP relay, pipe to a command, etc.), retry rules that govern how failed deliveries are re-attempted, rewrite rules for address rewriting, and authenticators for SMTP AUTH. Binding all of this together is Exim's own string-expansion language, a compact templating and conditional syntax embedded throughout the configuration file that lets administrators build very granular, condition-based mail policy without writing external code, while a local_scan() C API and embedded Perl support allow deeper custom logic when needed. Exim also exposes a content-scanning framework used to hook in third-party antivirus and antispam engines such as ClamAV and SpamAssassin. One notable architectural trade-off, acknowledged by Hazel himself, is that unlike Postfix or qmail, Exim has no central queue manager process; each Exim process independently scans the spool directory, which the project's own documentation admits performs less well than competitors when the mail queue grows very large.

Because Exim is licensed under the GNU General Public License (GPL-2.0-or-later), there is no pricing tier, subscription, or paid edition - the source code, binaries, and full documentation are free for anyone to download, modify, and redistribute, and this has never changed since 1995. Organizations that want commercial-grade support typically contract independent consultancies (several of which are long-time Exim maintainers) rather than paying Exim itself, since there is no vendor entity to bill. Infrastructure costs for the project (hosting, CI, mailing lists) are covered informally by sponsor organizations such as Mythic Beasts, MacStadium, and Schlittermann rather than through product revenue.

Exim's target users are almost entirely technical: Linux/Unix system administrators, web hosting companies, internet service providers, and university IT departments that need a highly configurable, self-hosted mail relay rather than an end-user email client or a hosted SaaS inbox. Its single largest real-world distribution channel is the web hosting industry, where it ships as the default MTA inside cPanel/WHM, one of the most widely deployed hosting control panels globally; this alone accounts for a large share of its installed base. It is also the default MTA on Debian Linux (and thus many Debian-derived distributions), is used heavily by UK academic institutions including the University of Cambridge that created it, and underpins the GNU Mailman mailing-list manager. A March 2023 survey by E-Soft, Inc. estimated that roughly 59 percent of publicly reachable mail servers on the internet were running Exim, ahead of Postfix (roughly 30 percent) and Sendmail (roughly 4 percent), making it, by that measurement, the most widely deployed MTA on the public internet, even though it is far less visible to end users than webmail brands.

Exim's principal competitors are other open-source MTAs - primarily Postfix, which is generally regarded as easier to configure securely out of the box and uses a more modular, multi-process queue architecture, and Sendmail, the older and now less dominant MTA that inspired Exim's single-binary privilege model. Beyond the open-source world, it is sometimes compared against commercial or enterprise mail platforms such as Microsoft Exchange, Zimbra, or high-volume commercial relays like PowerMTA, though those products target different use cases (integrated groupware or bulk-sending appliances rather than a bare, scriptable relay MTA). Exim differentiates itself primarily through the depth and flexibility of its configuration language: its ACL system allows extremely granular per-connection, per-recipient, and per-content policy decisions, and its string-expansion syntax lets administrators encode complex conditional logic directly in configuration rather than needing external plugins, which is one reason it remains the mailer of choice for hosting control panels that must support enormous numbers of heterogeneous customer domains from one shared installation.

Security history is a significant and well-documented part of Exim's public profile. Since its version-4 redesign around 2000, the project has disclosed several serious remote-code-execution vulnerabilities. The best known, CVE-2019-10149 (nicknamed 'The Return of the WIZard' by researchers at Qualys), affected versions 4.87 through 4.91 and stemmed from improper validation of a recipient address in the deliver_message() function; it allowed local privilege escalation to root and, in some configurations, remote code execution, and was actively exploited in the wild by a Linux worm within weeks of disclosure, with Shodan at the time showing millions of internet-facing Exim instances still running vulnerable versions. Earlier, in 2021, a cluster of vulnerabilities collectively referred to as '21Nails' were disclosed by Qualys researchers, covering both local and remote exploitation paths across many supported versions. In response to a serious issue years earlier, Exim maintainers shipped a security lockdown in release 4.73 that intentionally broke backward compatibility with some existing configurations - something the project's own documentation describes as one of the very rare occasions Exim has done so, given its strong general commitment to configuration stability across releases. These incidents mean that, more than most infrastructure software, Exim deployments require diligent patch management, and this history is a frequent talking point in comparisons against Postfix, which has a comparatively lighter public CVE history.

In terms of product evolution, Exim's major structural break was the version 3-to-4 rewrite around 2000, which introduced the router/transport separation still in use today and set the architecture that has been incrementally extended ever since (adding TLS, DKIM, DMARC, ARC, and DANE support in later 4.9x releases, and the PCRE2 migration in 2021). Development today happens in the open on GitHub under the Exim organization, governed informally by the long-standing maintainer group through public mailing lists rather than a corporate roadmap, and releases in the 4.9x branch continue to ship periodic security and feature updates. For a directory audience, Exim is best understood not as a competitor to hosted email services like Gmail or Zoho Mail, but as foundational, invisible plumbing: the free, battle-tested, extremely configurable MTA that a large share of the web's shared-hosting infrastructure and Debian-based servers run on by default.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Exim is a free, open-source mail transfer agent (MTA) that runs on Unix-like operating systems, handling the routing, filtering, and delivery of email between servers. It was created in 1995 by Philip Hazel at the University of Cambridge Computing Service as a more flexible successor to the older Smail-3 mailer, and it is distributed under the GNU General Public License (GPL-2.0-or-later).

Rather than being a consumer-facing app, Exim operates behind the scenes: it is the software that shared-hosting control panels, ISPs, and Linux distributions rely on to actually send and receive SMTP mail. It is the default MTA on Debian Linux and is bundled with cPanel/WHM, one of the most widely used hosting control panels in the world.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Exim's configuration is split into access control lists (ACLs), routers, transports, retry rules, rewrite rules, and authenticators, giving administrators granular control over exactly how each message is accepted, routed, and delivered. A built-in string-expansion language lets policy logic be expressed directly in the configuration file, and a content-scanning framework allows easy integration with antivirus and antispam engines such as ClamAV and SpamAssassin.

It supports modern email authentication and encryption standards including TLS, SMTP AUTH, DKIM, DMARC, ARC, and DANE, and offers extensibility through a local_scan() C API and embedded Perl support for custom logic beyond what the configuration language alone can express.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Exim is completely free and has no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensed editions; the GPL license guarantees the source code and binaries remain free to use, modify, and redistribute indefinitely. There is no vendor entity selling Exim, so there are no official support contracts - organizations that want commercial-grade help typically hire independent consultancies, some staffed by long-time Exim maintainers, rather than paying a central company.

Project infrastructure such as hosting and continuous integration is covered by volunteer sponsor organizations (for example Mythic Beasts, MacStadium, and Schlittermann) rather than by product revenue, which is typical for community-governed open-source infrastructure projects of this kind.

features.title: Access control lists (ACLs)
features.description: Fine-grained rules evaluated at each stage of an SMTP conversation decide whether to accept, reject, or defer a message, enabling detailed anti-spam and anti-abuse policy.

features.title: Routers and transports
features.description: A modular pipeline of routers (deciding how an address should be handled) and transports (defining how delivery actually happens) gives administrators precise control over mail flow.

features.title: String expansion language
features.description: A compact, condition-aware templating syntax embedded throughout the configuration file lets policy and routing logic be written declaratively without external scripts.

features.title: Content scanning framework
features.description: Built-in hooks make it straightforward to integrate third-party antivirus and antispam engines such as ClamAV and SpamAssassin directly into the mail pipeline.

features.title: TLS and modern auth support
features.description: Native support for TLS encryption, SMTP AUTH, DKIM, DMARC, ARC, and DANE helps deployments meet current email authentication and transport-security expectations.

features.title: PCRE-based regex matching
features.description: Exim uses the PCRE2 (Perl Compatible Regular Expressions) library, originally created by its own author, for powerful pattern matching throughout routing and filtering rules.

features.title: local_scan() and Perl extensibility
features.description: A C-level local_scan() API and optional embedded Perl interpreter allow administrators to add custom scanning or routing logic beyond the standard configuration language.

features.title: Cross-platform Unix deployment
features.description: Runs natively across Unix-like systems including Linux and BSD variants, with a Cygwin-based port available for Microsoft Windows environments.

pros: Completely free under the GPL with no paid tiers, licensing fees, or vendor lock-in.
Extremely configurable ACL and routing system that supports very granular, per-domain mail policy, which is why it underpins large multi-tenant hosting platforms.
Long track record and huge installed base, including being the default MTA on Debian and the mailer bundled with cPanel/WHM.
Actively maintained on GitHub by an experienced volunteer team with regular security and feature releases.
cons: Has a notable history of serious security vulnerabilities, including remote-code-execution flaws, that require diligent and timely patching.
Lacks a central queue manager, which its own documentation admits can hurt performance under very large mail queues compared to Postfix.
Configuration syntax and string-expansion language have a steep learning curve for administrators new to Exim.
No official vendor or paid support contract exists; commercial help must come from third-party consultancies.

faqs.question: What is Exim?
faqs.answer: Exim is a free, open-source mail transfer agent (MTA) that routes and delivers email on Unix-like systems. It was created in 1995 by Philip Hazel at the University of Cambridge and is licensed under the GNU General Public License.

faqs.question: Is Exim free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Exim has always been free under the GPL-2.0-or-later license, with no paid editions, subscription tiers, or usage fees. Anyone can download, modify, and redistribute it.

faqs.question: Who maintains Exim today?
faqs.answer: Philip Hazel retired from Cambridge in 2007, and maintenance passed to a volunteer group known as the Exim Maintainers, including contributors such as Heiko Schlittermann and Jeremy Harris, who coordinate development on GitHub and via public mailing lists.

faqs.question: How does Exim compare to Postfix?
faqs.answer: Both are free, GPL-licensed MTAs, but Postfix uses a modular multi-process queue manager that generally handles very large queues more gracefully, while Exim offers a more flexible single-binary configuration language favored by hosting control panels for granular, per-domain policy.

faqs.question: Why is Exim so widely used in web hosting?
faqs.answer: Exim ships as the default mail server in cPanel/WHM, one of the most widely deployed hosting control panels, and is also the default MTA on Debian Linux, which together give it one of the largest installed bases of any MTA on the public internet.

faqs.question: Has Exim had major security vulnerabilities?
faqs.answer: Yes. Notable examples include CVE-2019-10149, a remote-code-execution flaw actively exploited by a Linux worm in 2019, and the '21Nails' cluster of vulnerabilities disclosed in 2021, making prompt patching important for any Exim deployment.

enrichment.short_description: Expensify is a cloud-based expense management platform that combines receipt-scanning software with a corporate card, travel booking and bill pay, letting businesses automate expense reports, approvals and reimbursements from a single app.
enrichment.starting_price: Free for individuals; Collect plan starts at $5 per member per month
enrichment.seo_title: Expensify Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Expensify review 2026: SmartScan receipts, the Expensify Card, Travel and Bill Pay. See real Collect and Control pricing, features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Expensify is a cloud-based expense management and corporate spend platform built around automated receipt scanning, expense report creation, approval workflows and reimbursement. The company was founded in 2008 by David Barrett, who first pitched the concept at TechCrunch50 as 'Expensify: The Corporate Card for the Masses,' framing it as a way to simplify the tedious, manual process of tracking business receipts and filing expense reports. Barrett, a University of Michigan graduate and serial entrepreneur who had previously worked on peer-to-peer technology at Red Swoosh, built the company's early technology around one core insight: expense reporting was broken because it forced employees to manually re-type data from paper receipts. Expensify's response was SmartScan, one of the first receipt-scanning technologies in the expense management industry, which uses optical character recognition (OCR) and machine learning to extract merchant, date, amount and category data from a photographed receipt and automatically populate an expense line item. The company is headquartered in Portland, Oregon, in the historic former First National Bank Building, and has maintained a significant presence in Michigan as well, reflecting Barrett's roots and an early partnership with Gogebic Community College to expand technical operations outside the West Coast.

Expensify grew through a series of early venture funding rounds, raising roughly 1 million dollars in 2009, 5.7 million dollars in 2010, and additional capital in 2014 and 2015, with the 2015 round coinciding with the launch of Expensify Ventures, an internal fund. Over the following decade the company expanded well beyond receipt scanning into a full spend-management suite. In November 2021, Expensify went public on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the ticker symbol EXFY, pricing its initial public offering at 27 dollars per share and raising net proceeds of roughly 57.5 million dollars after underwriting costs, with joint bookrunners J.P. Morgan Securities, Citigroup Global Markets and BofA Securities. The IPO valued the company at approximately 3.87 billion dollars at debut. Since going public, Expensify has published its financials as a listed company; for the 2022 fiscal year it reported revenue of about 169 million dollars alongside a net loss of roughly 27 million dollars, and its stock price has fallen sharply from IPO levels, reflecting broader pressure on expense-management valuations amid growing competition from newer, card-led spend platforms.

Technically, Expensify operates as a workflow engine layered on top of receipt capture. Employees submit expenses by photographing a paper receipt with the mobile app, forwarding an emailed receipt to a dedicated Expensify inbox, or connecting a bank or credit card feed so that transactions import automatically. SmartScan then extracts the relevant fields, applies the company's expense policy rules (per diem limits, category restrictions, required fields) and flags violations before a report is ever submitted, which reduces back-and-forth between employees and finance teams. Approved reports route through configurable, multi-level approval workflows and can be reimbursed directly through Expensify's integrated payment rails, typically arriving in an employee's bank account within one to two business days for domestic transfers. The platform integrates with major accounting and ERP systems including QuickBooks Online and Desktop, Xero, NetSuite, Sage Intacct and FinancialForce, syncing chart-of-accounts data, tax codes and vendor records so that finance teams do not have to re-key transactions. On top of the core expense-report engine, Expensify has layered three additional products: the Expensify Card, a corporate charge card that offers cash-back rewards (1 percent standard, up to 2 percent at very high spend volumes) and lets administrators set category-level spending controls and freeze cards instantly from the app; Expensify Travel, a built-in booking tool for flights, hotels, cars and rail that in 2026 added consolidated travel billing so companies can pay for all employee travel on a single monthly statement rather than issuing cards to every traveler; and Bill Pay, which extends the same approval and payment infrastructure to vendor invoices and accounts payable.

Expensify's pricing structure separates individual use from company workspaces. Individuals can use Expensify for free, with up to 25 SmartScans per month, unlimited manual expense entry, distance tracking and CSV or spreadsheet export, making it suitable for freelancers and sole proprietors who need basic receipt capture without collaboration features. For teams, the company restructured its pricing in April 2025 to simplify what had become a complicated set of discount tiers and commitments. The Collect plan now costs a flat 5 dollars per member per month with no annual contract required, and is aimed at small businesses that need basic approval and reimbursement structure without deep integrations. The Control plan, aimed at larger or more compliance-sensitive organizations, adds multi-level approvals, ERP integrations, HR and payroll connections, multiple corporate card connections, custom expense rules and SAML/SSO; it is priced at 9 dollars per active member per month with an annual commitment when paired with the Expensify Card, 18 dollars per member per month annually without the card, or 36 dollars per member per month on a pay-per-use, no-commitment basis. In both team plans, 'active member' generally means a user who creates, submits, approves, reimburses or exports a report in a given billing period, rather than every seat provisioned.

Expensify's target audience spans a wide range of company sizes, from solo freelancers and small businesses on the free or Collect tiers up to mid-market and larger companies that need Control's compliance and integration depth. Its most direct competitors include SAP Concur, a legacy enterprise incumbent known for deep ERP and travel-policy integration but criticized for a complex interface, per-transaction pricing and slow innovation; Ramp and Brex, newer card-led spend management platforms that bundle corporate cards, bill pay and expense automation with often more aggressive free tiers and automation-focused positioning; and smaller point solutions like Zoho Expense and Rydoo. Within this landscape Expensify differentiates itself through its long history and brand recognition as the pioneer of automated receipt scanning, its simplicity relative to Concur, and its willingness to offer a genuinely free individual tier, though it has faced increasing competitive pressure from card-issuing challengers that can subsidize software costs through interchange revenue. Notable events in the company's history include a 2017 privacy incident in which some Expensify receipt images were found to have been reviewed by human contractors through Amazon's Mechanical Turk service for data-verification purposes, which drew public scrutiny of the company's data-handling practices, as well as community and civic initiatives such as funding the renovation of a Portland food-cart pod. As of the most recent public disclosures, Expensify employed roughly 130 to 140 people, a comparatively lean headcount for a publicly traded software company serving a large global customer base, reflecting the company's historical emphasis on automation and self-serve adoption over a large direct sales organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Expensify is an expense management platform founded in 2008 by David Barrett and headquartered in Portland, Oregon. The company built its reputation on SmartScan, an early receipt-scanning technology that uses OCR to automatically extract merchant, date and amount data from photographed receipts, eliminating manual data entry from the expense reporting process.

Beyond expense reports, Expensify now offers a broader spend management suite that includes the Expensify Card (a corporate charge card with cash-back rewards and spending controls), Expensify Travel (flight, hotel, car and rail booking with consolidated monthly billing) and Bill Pay for vendor invoices. The company went public on the Nasdaq Global Select Market in November 2021 under the ticker EXFY.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core of the product is automated receipt capture: employees can photograph receipts in the mobile app, forward emailed receipts to a dedicated inbox, or sync a bank or card feed so transactions import automatically, with SmartScan extracting the data needed to build an expense line item.

Configurable, multi-level approval workflows route submitted reports to the right managers, expense policy rules flag out-of-policy spending before submission, and integrated reimbursement can deposit funds into an employee's bank account within one to two business days. Accounting integrations with QuickBooks, Xero, NetSuite, Sage Intacct and FinancialForce keep finance systems in sync without manual re-entry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Individuals can use Expensify for free, with up to 25 SmartScans per month, unlimited manual entry and CSV export, making it a viable option for freelancers and sole proprietors who do not need team workflows.

For company workspaces, Expensify restructured pricing in April 2025 around two tiers: Collect at a flat 5 dollars per member per month with no annual commitment, and Control (which adds multi-level approvals, ERP and payroll integrations, custom rules and SSO) starting at 9 dollars per active member per month with an annual commitment and the Expensify Card, rising to 18 dollars per member per month annually without the card, or 36 dollars per member per month on a no-commitment, pay-per-use basis.

enrichment.short_description: ExpressVPN is a consumer and small-business virtual private network service that encrypts internet traffic and masks IP addresses across 105+ countries, built on its proprietary open-source Lightway protocol and RAM-only TrustedServer infrastructure.
enrichment.seo_title: ExpressVPN Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ExpressVPN review 2026: pricing tiers, Lightway protocol, TrustedServer tech, audited no-logs policy, 214 server locations, and how it compares to NordVPN and Surfshark.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
ExpressVPN is a virtual private network (VPN) service that encrypts a user's internet connection and routes it through a remote server to hide the user's IP address, location, and browsing activity from internet service providers, network operators, and websites. The product is aimed at everyday consumers, privacy-conscious professionals, and travelers who need to secure connections on public Wi-Fi, bypass geographic content restrictions, or reduce the amount of browsing data visible to third parties. Technically, ExpressVPN operates a global network of physical and virtual servers and offers native applications for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android, along with browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, and Edge, router firmware for popular consumer routers, and its own purpose-built router, Aircove. All apps connect through one of several tunneling protocols, most commonly ExpressVPN's own Lightway protocol, alongside industry-standard alternatives such as WireGuard, OpenVPN, and IKEv2, giving users a choice between speed-optimized and broadly compatible connection methods.

The company was founded in 2009 by Peter Burchhardt and Dan Pomerantz, two serial entrepreneurs and Wharton School alumni, who built the service around the idea of giving ordinary internet users military-grade encryption without requiring technical expertise. ExpressVPN is incorporated in Tortola, British Virgin Islands, a jurisdiction that does not impose mandatory data-retention laws on internet or telecommunications companies, which the company has cited as a structural reason it can credibly claim not to log user activity. Over the following decade the company grew into one of the most recognized brands in consumer VPN software, reporting more than four million active users and roughly 279 million dollars in 2020 revenue at the time it changed ownership. In September 2021, ExpressVPN was acquired by Kape Technologies plc, a London Stock Exchange-listed digital privacy and security company, in a deal valued at approximately 936 million dollars, which at the time was the largest recorded acquisition in the VPN industry. The acquisition brought ExpressVPN into the same corporate family as other VPN brands Kape had previously acquired, including CyberGhost VPN, ZenMate, and Private Internet Access, expanding Kape's combined subscriber base to more than six million users and its combined workforce to roughly 720 employees at the time; ExpressVPN has continued to operate under its own brand, app, and support infrastructure rather than being merged into a single combined product.

A defining piece of ExpressVPN's technical architecture is TrustedServer, a server design the company introduced in 2019 in which every VPN server runs entirely in RAM rather than on a hard disk. Because the operating system and all applications are loaded fresh from a secure, read-only image on every boot and no data is written to a persistent disk, any information generated during a session is wiped the moment a server restarts, which the company argues meaningfully reduces the risk that sensitive data could persist, be seized, or be misconfigured on a physical machine. This architecture has been reviewed multiple times by outside auditors, including PwC Switzerland in 2019 and KPMG in subsequent years, with the most recent KPMG review of TrustedServer and the company's no-logs privacy policy, completed in 2025, reporting no exceptions. In total, ExpressVPN has published more than twenty independent third-party audit reports covering its server infrastructure, no-logs claims, browser extensions, and named security researchers or firms including Cure53, F-Secure, and Praetorian, a level of external verification the company markets as a key differentiator from VPN providers that make unaudited privacy claims.

The second core piece of technical differentiation is Lightway, ExpressVPN's own VPN protocol, built primarily in the Rust programming language and open-sourced on GitHub so outside researchers can inspect its code. Lightway was designed to have a small codebase relative to older protocols such as OpenVPN, which the company says reduces the attack surface and allows faster connection times and better performance on mobile networks that frequently switch between Wi-Fi and cellular data. Lightway has been independently audited multiple times, including two code and penetration test audits by Cure53 and a review by Praetorian, and the company has since introduced a variant called Lightway Turbo that uses multi-lane tunneling to push more data through a connection simultaneously for higher throughput. Alongside Lightway, the apps support WireGuard and OpenVPN for compatibility, standard AES-256 encryption, a kill switch feature branded Network Lock that blocks internet traffic if the VPN connection drops, and split tunneling that lets users route only selected apps or websites through the VPN tunnel.

As of mid-2026, ExpressVPN operates a network described by the company as its broadest ever, spanning 214 distinct, app-selectable server locations across 113 countries, a network it has continued to expand through periodic announcements of new locations. This is smaller in raw server count than some rivals; competitor NordVPN, for instance, advertises roughly 9,300 servers across 137 countries, and CyberGhost, also owned by Kape, advertises more than 9,000 servers, though ExpressVPN has generally positioned itself around consistency and per-location performance rather than raw server count.

Pricing is structured as three tiered subscription plans, introduced as ExpressVPN moved away from a single flat plan toward a Basic, Advanced, and Pro tier system. The Basic plan starts at 3.49 dollars per month on a two-year commitment, 4.99 dollars per month on an annual plan, or 12.99 dollars per month billed monthly, and covers the core VPN functionality across up to 10 simultaneous device connections. The Advanced plan adds support for up to 12 devices along with ExpressVPN Keys, a built-in password manager, more aggressive ad and tracker blocking, identity monitoring for US-based customers, and a few days of complimentary eSIM mobile data, priced from roughly 5.99 dollars per month on an annual plan up to 13.99 dollars per month billed monthly. The Pro plan extends device support to 14 simultaneous connections and adds a dedicated static IP address, identity theft protection, credit monitoring, and a data removal service for US customers, priced from roughly 7.49 dollars per month annually up to 19.99 dollars per month billed monthly. All plans are backed by a 30-day money-back guarantee for first-time customers who purchase directly through ExpressVPN, though purchases made through the Apple App Store or Google Play Store are instead subject to those platforms' own refund policies. ExpressVPN also offers a 3-day free trial exclusively on iOS and Android, requiring a linked payment method through the relevant app store.

ExpressVPN's target audience spans several overlapping groups: everyday consumers who want to protect their browsing on public Wi-Fi and reduce tracking by internet service providers, travelers and expatriates who want to access streaming libraries, news sites, or banking services that are geo-restricted in the country they are physically in, and remote workers or small business owners who want an easy, non-technical way to add encryption to their internet connection without managing enterprise VPN infrastructure. The company markets itself less toward large enterprise IT departments, which more commonly rely on dedicated business VPN or zero-trust network access products, and more toward individuals and households, a positioning reflected in its consumer-style pricing, app design, and marketing.

In the competitive landscape, ExpressVPN is most frequently compared against NordVPN, Surfshark, CyberGhost, Private Internet Access, and ProtonVPN. Reviewers generally note that ExpressVPN is priced at the higher end of the consumer VPN market and does not offer an unlimited-device plan the way Surfshark does, and that NordVPN often edges it out on raw connection speed benchmarks and total server count. ExpressVPN's differentiation instead rests on the combination of its own open-sourced, independently audited protocol, its RAM-only server architecture with a long public audit history, broad platform support including its own Aircove hardware router, and a reputation built over more than fifteen years of operation predating many newer entrants to the category.

The company has also faced public scrutiny at points in its history. In 2021, ExpressVPN's then chief information officer, Daniel Gericke, agreed to pay a 335,000 dollar fine to US authorities in connection with prior work conducting hacking operations for the United Arab Emirates government before joining the company, a matter unrelated to ExpressVPN's own products but reported alongside coverage of the company given his executive role. Separately, security researchers identified a DNS leak affecting some Windows app versions between May 2022 and February 2024 for users relying on split tunneling, which the company patched after disclosure. ExpressVPN apps were also among those removed from Apple's China App Store in 2017 following a government request, illustrating the jurisdictional pressure VPN providers can face in markets that restrict circumvention tools. The company has continued to invest in transparency measures since then, including a Digital Security Lab launched in 2020 for original privacy and security research, and membership in the VPN Trust Initiative, an industry body focused on establishing baseline standards for VPN provider conduct.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ExpressVPN is a virtual private network service, founded in 2009 by Peter Burchhardt and Dan Pomerantz, that encrypts a device's internet traffic and routes it through a remote server to hide the user's real IP address and location. The company is incorporated in Tortola, British Virgin Islands, a jurisdiction without mandatory data-retention requirements, which underpins its no-logs privacy claims.

Since September 2021, ExpressVPN has operated as a subsidiary of Kape Technologies plc, a London Stock Exchange-listed digital security company that acquired it for approximately 936 million dollars, at the time the largest deal in VPN industry history. ExpressVPN continues to run as an independently branded product alongside sister Kape brands CyberGhost, ZenMate, and Private Internet Access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The service is built around two proprietary technologies: Lightway, an open-source VPN protocol written primarily in Rust and independently audited by Cure53 and Praetorian, and TrustedServer, a RAM-only diskless server architecture that wipes all data on every reboot and has been audited by PwC and KPMG. As of mid-2026, ExpressVPN operates 214 app-selectable server locations across 113 countries.

Beyond the core VPN tunnel, the apps include a kill switch called Network Lock, split tunneling, broad platform coverage across Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android, browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, and Edge, support for popular consumer routers, and a dedicated hardware router called Aircove. Higher-tier plans add a password manager (Keys), ad and tracker blocking, identity monitoring, and a dedicated IP address.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ExpressVPN sells three tiered subscription plans: Basic, Advanced, and Pro, each available on monthly, annual, or two-year billing cycles, with the lowest effective price on the two-year Basic plan at 3.49 dollars per month. Advanced adds a password manager, more aggressive tracking protection, and identity monitoring, while Pro adds a dedicated IP address, identity theft protection, credit monitoring, and a data removal service, alongside higher simultaneous device limits at each tier (10, 12, and 14 devices respectively).

All direct purchases are covered by a 30-day money-back guarantee for first-time customers, and a 3-day free trial is available on iOS and Android through the relevant app store. Purchases made through Apple's App Store or Google Play are instead governed by those platforms' own refund policies rather than ExpressVPN's guarantee.

faqs.answer: ExpressVPN states it does not log browsing activity, connection timestamps, or traffic data. faqs.question: How much does ExpressVPN cost?
faqs.answer: ExpressVPN offers three tiers. Basic starts at 3.49 dollars per month on a two-year plan (or 12.99 dollars per month billed monthly), Advanced starts around 5.99 dollars per month annually, and Pro starts around 7.49 dollars per month annually, with higher monthly-billed rates for each tier.

faqs.question: Can ExpressVPN be used for streaming services like Netflix?
faqs.answer: Yes, ExpressVPN is commonly used to access geographically restricted streaming libraries and is regularly tested by reviewers against major platforms, though which specific libraries unblock can change over time as streaming services update their detection methods.

faqs.question: Does ExpressVPN offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: ExpressVPN offers a 3-day free trial on iOS and Android through the app store, requiring a linked payment method. On other platforms, new customers can instead rely on the 30-day money-back guarantee for a full refund if purchased directly from ExpressVPN.

faqs.question: Who owns ExpressVPN?
faqs.answer: ExpressVPN has been owned by Kape Technologies plc, a London Stock Exchange-listed digital security company, since it acquired ExpressVPN in September 2021 for approximately 936 million dollars. Kape also owns the VPN brands CyberGhost, ZenMate, and Private Internet Access.

enrichment.short_description: F-Droid is a free and open-source software (FOSS) catalog and installer client for Android, offering a privacy-respecting alternative to Google Play by building every app itself from public source code and flagging tracking or advertising 'anti-features'.
enrichment.seo_title: F-Droid Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: F-Droid review 2026: how the free, open-source Android app repository works, its build-from-source model, pricing (free), pros, cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
F-Droid is a free and open-source software (FOSS) catalog and client application for the Android operating system that functions as an alternative to the Google Play Store. Rather than accepting pre-compiled binaries directly from developers the way commercial app stores do, F-Droid builds nearly every application it distributes from publicly available source code on its own build servers, then signs the resulting package with its own keys before adding it to the repository. This source-first model is the defining technical and philosophical characteristic of the project: it treats the published source code, not the developer's claims, as the ground truth for what an app actually does, and it makes the entire toolchain used to produce the repository openly available so that anyone can inspect it or stand up a competing repository using the same software.

The project was founded on September 29, 2010 by British software developer Ciaran Gultnieks. In later interviews, including one given to the Free Software Foundation in 2014, Gultnieks explained that he was motivated to start F-Droid by frustration with 'lock-down, lock-in and general nefarious behavior from software' running on mobile phones, at a time when Android was still young and the idea of a fully open, Google-independent app ecosystem was largely unexplored. The client application itself was originally derived from code shared with the Aptoide project, and development was hosted first on the AGPL-licensed Gitorious platform before the project migrated its repositories to GitLab around 2015 as Gitorious wound down.

Governance has evolved considerably since F-Droid's founding. The project was initially operated by an English nonprofit company, F-Droid Limited, which provided a legal and financial structure for the effort. By 2021 F-Droid Limited was in the process of winding down, and today the project is legally represented through the Commons Conservancy, an umbrella foundation that provides fiscal sponsorship and legal continuity to open-source projects without requiring each one to maintain its own corporate entity. Day-to-day work is carried out by a distributed team of core volunteers, a small number of paid contributors funded through donations via platforms such as OpenCollective and Liberapay, and a broad international community of packagers, translators, and testers who maintain metadata for individual apps in the public 'fdroiddata' repository.

Technically, the F-Droid ecosystem is composed of several distinct but interlocking components. The F-Droid client is a Java-based Android application that lets users browse, search, install, and receive automatic update notifications for apps without creating any account and without any Google Play Services dependency. The server side consists of 'fdroidserver', a set of Python-based tools that fetch source code from each app's repository, drive the build process in an isolated, reproducible environment, and generate the signed index files the client consumes; the public-facing website itself is built with Jekyll. Because F-Droid controls the entire build pipeline, it can support 'reproducible builds,' a verification technique in which independent third parties recompile the same source code and confirm the output matches byte-for-byte what F-Droid published, closing the trust gap that exists when a store simply re-signs a binary handed to it by a developer. In more recent years F-Droid has also added support for developer-signed builds validated through reproducible-build verification, giving developers more flexibility while preserving auditability.

A hallmark feature of the F-Droid client is its 'anti-features' system, which labels each app listing with tags such as advertising, tracking, non-free network services, non-free dependencies, or non-free add-ons whenever the build process or metadata review detects them, giving privacy-conscious users clear, unavoidable disclosure before they install anything. The client also supports advanced distribution scenarios aimed at users in restrictive or bandwidth-constrained environments: it can route traffic through HTTP proxies or Tor onion services, and it supports offline sharing of apps between devices over local Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, which is particularly valuable in regions with unreliable internet access or active censorship of app stores. Users are not limited to F-Droid's own main repository either; the client can add third-party repositories, and a broader ecosystem has grown around this extensibility, including large community-run mirrors such as IzzyOnDroid and dedicated repositories operated by organizations like the Guardian Project.

F-Droid's primary audience consists of privacy- and freedom-conscious Android users, security researchers, and the broader de-Googled device community, including users of custom Android distributions such as GrapheneOS, CalyxOS, LineageOS, and /e/OS who want a way to install verified open-source software without relying on Google Mobile Services. It is also widely used by open-source developers themselves as a low-friction distribution channel that reaches an audience already predisposed to FOSS values, and by organizations and NGOs operating in regions where Google Play access is unreliable or where distributing software through a Google-controlled channel carries surveillance or censorship risk.

In the broader app-distribution market, F-Droid occupies a clearly differentiated niche relative to its main points of comparison. Google Play remains vastly larger, listing on the order of a couple million applications against F-Droid's several thousand, and relies primarily on automated scanning plus developer self-attestation rather than from-source builds, trading catalog breadth and update convenience for weaker transparency guarantees. Aurora Store, an unofficial FOSS client for the Play Store itself, lets users fetch official Play-hosted APKs anonymously without a Google account, but by design it still talks to Google's servers and inherits whatever tracking exists in the underlying apps, making it a convenience tool for reaching Play-exclusive software rather than a trust-anchored alternative. APKPure and similar third-party APK aggregators offer an even larger catalog than either F-Droid or Aurora Store, including proprietary and often modified or repackaged apps, but provide essentially none of F-Droid's source verification, reproducible-build guarantees, or anti-feature disclosure, which makes them a materially higher-risk option from a security and provenance standpoint. Within the FOSS app-store space itself, F-Droid is also the de facto trust anchor that alternative clients such as Droid-ify and Neo Store, as well as auto-update tools like Obtainium, are built to consume.

Several notable episodes mark the project's history and reputation. In 2012, F-Droid removed an app after discovering it leaked personal information, an early demonstration of its willingness to police its own catalog. The project has stated that no malware was found in the official f-droid.org repository during its first seven years of operation, a claim frequently cited by security-focused reviewers, though in 2022 researchers identified a set of previously distributed apps that contained known vulnerabilities, prompting tightened review practices. F-Droid has also navigated governance controversy, including a 2019 public statement on the neutrality of free software addressing criticism of past moderation decisions, and a 2016 finding by the Replicant operating system project that F-Droid's repository did not fully comply with the GNU Free System Distribution Guidelines, a disagreement that eventually led Replicant to drop F-Droid from its default repositories in mid-2022. Independent of that dispute, GrapheneOS opted to build its own distribution mechanism rather than integrate F-Droid directly, citing a desire for a higher security and robustness bar than F-Droid's model provides for its own curated app set. Most recently, in early 2026 F-Droid published an open letter opposing Google's plans to require mandatory developer identity verification for Android app installation, framing the policy as a threat to the sideloading and alternative-repository model that F-Droid itself depends on, and helping organize the community campaign at keepandroidopen.org.

Product-wise, F-Droid has grown steadily rather than dramatically: the main repository now serves well over four thousand actively maintained apps, with several thousand more retained in an archive repository for older or discontinued packages, and the client interface itself is localized into more than thirty languages, reflecting a genuinely global volunteer contributor base. Press coverage over the years, from CNET's 2019 assessment of its security posture to later How-To Geek and Popular Science pieces framing it as an essential tool for 'de-Googling' an Android phone, has been consistently positive on trust and privacy grounds while acknowledging the tradeoffs of a smaller catalog and a less automated update experience compared with Google Play. Taken together, F-Droid functions less like a conventional consumer app store and more like a piece of public infrastructure for the open-source Android ecosystem: a transparent, community-governed alternative distribution channel whose core value proposition, source-verified builds with no ads, no tracking, and no account requirement, has remained essentially unchanged since 2010 even as the surrounding governance structure, build tooling, and competitive landscape have all evolved considerably around it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: F-Droid is a free and open-source software (FOSS) app repository and Android client that acts as an alternative to the Google Play Store. Instead of accepting compiled binaries from developers, F-Droid builds nearly every listed app directly from its public source code on dedicated build servers and signs the result itself, giving users a verifiable chain from source to installed package.

Founded in 2010 by British developer Ciaran Gultnieks, the project is now a volunteer-driven nonprofit effort legally represented by the Commons Conservancy. It has no shareholders, no advertising business model, and no account requirement, funded instead through community donations via OpenCollective and Liberapay.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: F-Droid's client app lets users browse, install, and automatically update FOSS Android apps without a Google account or Google Play Services. Every listing displays 'anti-feature' tags for advertising, tracking, or non-free dependencies, so users know exactly what they are installing before they tap install.

The platform supports reproducible builds for independent verification, custom third-party repositories for extending the catalog, and offline distribution over local Wi-Fi or Bluetooth, which is especially useful in low-connectivity or censorship-affected regions. Traffic can also be routed through Tor onion services or HTTP proxies for users who need additional anonymity.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: F-Droid is entirely free to use for both end users and app developers; there is no paid tier, subscription, or in-app purchase anywhere in the product. The project itself is sustained by voluntary donations and a small number of grant-funded contributors rather than by revenue from users.

Because F-Droid also publishes the open-source tooling used to run its own repository, organizations or individuals can host their own independent F-Droid-compatible repository at no licensing cost, which is common among privacy-focused app developers and custom Android ROM communities.

features.title: Source-built apps
features.description: F-Droid compiles most apps itself directly from public source code on its own build servers rather than accepting pre-built binaries from developers, reducing the risk of hidden or tampered code.

features.title: Anti-features labeling
features.description: Every app listing discloses 'anti-features' such as advertising, tracking, or dependence on non-free software components, giving users clear disclosure before installing.

features.title: No account required
features.description: Apps can be browsed, downloaded, and installed from the F-Droid website or client without creating an account or providing any personal information.

features.title: Reproducible build verification
features.description: Independent third parties can recompile an app's published source and confirm the output matches F-Droid's signed binary byte-for-byte, closing the trust gap left by simple binary re-signing.

features.title: Custom and community repositories
features.description: The client supports adding third-party repository URLs, letting users pull in additional catalogs such as the Guardian Project's repository or large community mirrors like IzzyOnDroid.

features.title: Offline and local sharing
features.description: Apps can be shared directly between devices over local Wi-Fi or Bluetooth without an internet connection, useful in low-connectivity or censorship-affected regions.

features.title: Tor and proxy support
features.description: The client can route its traffic through Tor onion services or HTTP proxies, letting privacy-focused users browse and download anonymously.

features.title: Automatic update checking
features.description: Once an app is installed via F-Droid, the client checks for and notifies users of new versions without relying on Google Play Services.

pros: Every app is compiled from publicly auditable source code rather than a developer-supplied binary, giving a stronger provenance guarantee than most app stores.
Anti-feature labels make ads, tracking, and non-free dependencies visible up front, which is rare among mainstream app marketplaces.
Completely free with no account, no ads, and no Google Play Services dependency, making it a natural fit for privacy-focused and de-Googled Android setups.
Fully open governance and tooling mean anyone can audit the build process or stand up an independent, compatible repository.
cons: The catalog is far smaller than Google Play or third-party APK aggregators, so many mainstream and proprietary apps are simply unavailable.
Some apps lag behind their upstream releases because each update must pass through F-Droid's own build and review pipeline before it is published.
Installation requires enabling 'unknown sources' or sideloading permissions, which is a higher-friction and less familiar flow for non-technical users.
Source verification is best-effort rather than exhaustive, and the project has acknowledged past cases of apps later found to contain known vulnerabilities.

faqs.question: Is F-Droid free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. F-Droid is entirely free for both users and developers, with no subscriptions, in-app purchases, or account requirement. The project is funded through community donations rather than revenue from users.

faqs.question: Is F-Droid safe and legitimate?
faqs.answer: F-Droid is a long-running, well-known open-source project that builds most apps itself from public source code and flags tracking or advertising behavior in its listings. It has stated no malware was found in its official repository during its first seven years, though as with any software catalog users should still review individual app permissions and anti-feature tags.

faqs.question: How is F-Droid different from Google Play?
faqs.answer: Google Play accepts pre-compiled apps from developers and relies mainly on automated scanning, while F-Droid builds almost every app itself from source code on its own servers and signs the result, giving a more verifiable chain from source to installed package. F-Droid's catalog is also far smaller and limited to free and open-source software.

faqs.question: Do I need Google Play Services to use F-Droid?
faqs.answer: No. F-Droid and the apps distributed through its main repository are designed to run without Google Play Services, which is a key reason it is popular on custom Android distributions like GrapheneOS, CalyxOS, and LineageOS.

faqs.question: Can I add other app repositories to F-Droid?
faqs.answer: Yes. The F-Droid client supports adding third-party repository URLs, allowing users to pull in additional catalogs such as the Guardian Project's repository or large community mirrors like IzzyOnDroid alongside the default main repository.

faqs.question: Who runs and maintains F-Droid?
faqs.answer: F-Droid was founded in 2010 by Ciaran Gultnieks and is now a volunteer-run nonprofit project legally represented through the Commons Conservancy, staffed by a mix of core volunteers, a small number of paid contributors, and a broad international community of app packagers and translators.

enrichment.short_description: Factorial (FactorialHR) is a Barcelona-based all-in-one HR platform for small and mid-sized businesses, unifying payroll, time tracking, absence management, performance, recruitment and document management in one modular system.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start from around $8 per user per month
enrichment.seo_title: Factorial Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Factorial is a Barcelona-built HRIS for SMBs covering payroll, time tracking, absences and recruitment. See 2026 pricing, features and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Factorial, commercially known as FactorialHR, is a cloud-based human resources information system (HRIS) built for small and mid-sized businesses that want a single platform to run payroll, time tracking, absence management, performance reviews, recruitment and employee documentation instead of stitching together spreadsheets and point solutions. The product is organized around a core employee record system plus a set of modular 'hubs' that customers layer on top depending on which HR processes they want to digitize, an architecture that lets a five-person startup use only document management and e-signature while a five-hundred-person company runs full payroll, applicant tracking and IT asset management from the same account.

The company was founded on September 16, 2016 in Barcelona, Spain by Jordi Romero, Bernat Farrero and Pau Ramon Revilla, three Catalan entrepreneurs who had previously built and sold other technology companies before turning to the HR software space. The founding thesis was that HR tooling built for large enterprises, such as Workday and SAP SuccessFactors, was too complex and expensive for the small and mid-sized businesses that make up the vast majority of the European economy, and that a lighter-weight, self-service alternative could win that segment. Early traction came from digitizing manual HR paperwork for Spanish and broader European SMBs, and the company expanded from Barcelona into Madrid before pushing into Latin America and, briefly, North America.

Factorial's funding history traces its growth from a regional Spanish startup to a pan-European HR technology company. It raised a 16 million dollar Series A in 2020, described at the time as the largest funding round in Spain that year, to accelerate international expansion during the pandemic, a period in which the company also made parts of its software available free of charge to help businesses manage remote and furloughed workforces. In September 2021 it closed an 80 million dollar Series B at roughly a 530 million dollar valuation, opening offices in the United States and Brazil and positioning itself in press coverage as a 'Workday for SMBs.' In October 2022, Factorial closed a Series C of just over 120 million dollars led by investors including CRV and Creandum, pushing its valuation to approximately 1 billion dollars and making it one of Barcelona's first unicorn technology companies. In June 2026 the company announced a 150 million dollar Series D led by General Catalyst, with participation from existing backers including Atomico, that pushed its valuation above 2.5 billion dollars, reflecting continued growth in its subscription base and expansion of AI features across the product. As of the mid-2020s, Factorial reports serving more than 16,000 businesses across more than 90 countries, with a workforce of roughly 1,000 employees spread across offices in Barcelona, Madrid, Sao Paulo and Mexico City after a 2023-opened Miami office was closed in a later North American restructuring.

Technically, Factorial is delivered as a multi-tenant SaaS application accessed through a web browser and companion iOS and Android mobile apps, with no on-premises or self-hosted deployment option. The platform is structured around a central 'Factorial Core' that every customer receives, covering the employee directory, org charts, onboarding and offboarding workflows, payslip distribution, legally binding e-signature, fillable PDF forms and custom notifications. On top of Core, customers add individual hubs: a Time hub covering time off requests, time tracking and shift management; a Talent hub covering performance reviews, recruitment and applicant tracking, employee engagement surveys and training; a Finance hub covering expense management, procurement and project management; and an IT hub covering SaaS spend management, IT asset inventory, multi-OS mobile device management and endpoint detection and cybersecurity. Each hub is available in a standard and a more advanced tier, so pricing scales both with headcount and with how many hubs and tiers a company activates, rather than through a small number of fixed named plans. Factorial has increasingly layered artificial intelligence across these modules, including an AI-assisted applicant tracking system that helps personalize job postings and automate candidate communication, CV parsing, receipt OCR for expense capture, AI-generated meeting summaries, and a natural-language reporting assistant that lets HR managers ask plain-language questions and receive real-time analytics without building custom reports. The platform supports payroll and compliance workflows across multiple countries and legal entities, handles roughly 180 currencies, and integrates with common finance, communication and applicant sourcing tools through its API and app marketplace.

On pricing, Factorial offers a genuinely free plan limited to document management and digital signature features, useful for very small teams that only need a lightweight way to store and sign HR paperwork. Paid access begins with the Core plan, publicly quoted as starting around 8 dollars per user per month, which unlocks the employee directory, onboarding, payslips and e-signature at scale. Beyond Core, pricing becomes custom: businesses select which Time, Talent, Finance and IT hubs they need, choose between the standard and Enterprise variant of each, and receive a tailored per-employee monthly quote, with third-party aggregators citing typical bundled pricing in the roughly 6.90 to 14.45 dollar per user per month range depending on configuration. A free trial is available across the paid tiers so prospective customers can test hub combinations before committing to a contract.

Factorial's target users are HR managers, People Ops leads, office managers and founders at companies roughly between 10 and a few hundred employees who need HRIS, time and attendance, and light recruiting functionality but do not have the budget, headcount or complexity to justify an enterprise system like Workday or SAP SuccessFactors. It is particularly strong for European SMBs, given the platform's roots in Spanish and broader EU labor law compliance, multi-language payslips and works-council-style reporting, though it has expanded coverage into Latin America and other markets. Within the competitive landscape, Factorial is most frequently compared to Personio, a German competitor also focused on the European mid-market; BambooHR, a US-based HRIS strong on core HR and onboarding but historically weaker on built-in payroll; Rippling, a broader HR-IT-finance platform aimed more at US tech companies wanting unified employee operations and device management; and Gusto, a US-focused payroll-first platform popular with very small businesses. Reviewers and analysts frequently position Factorial as the most direct alternative to Personio for European buyers, citing comparable functional coverage, generally more transparent published starting pricing, and a broader IT-management hub that few HR-first competitors offer natively.

Notable facts about the company include its status as one of the earliest Barcelona-headquartered unicorns, its practice of offering free software access during the COVID-19 pandemic to help affected businesses manage workforce disruption, and its rapid four-round fundraising trajectory from Series A to Series D in roughly six years. Architecturally, the product's hub-and-core model reflects a broader trend among modern HR platforms toward composable, pay-for-what-you-use suites rather than the flat per-seat licensing common to older HRIS vendors, and its increasing investment in embedded AI, from applicant screening to natural-language analytics, mirrors the wider shift in HR technology toward AI-assisted decision support for time-constrained People teams at growing companies.

paragraphs: Factorial is a cloud-based all-in-one HR platform built in Barcelona for small and mid-sized businesses that want to replace spreadsheets and disconnected point tools with a single system for payroll, time tracking, absence management, performance, recruitment and document management.

Founded in 2016 by Jordi Romero, Bernat Farrero and Pau Ramon Revilla, the company grew from a Spanish HR digitization tool into a European unicorn, raising a Series D in 2026 at a valuation above 2.5 billion dollars and now serving more than 16,000 businesses across over 90 countries.

Customers then add modular hubs for Time (time off, time tracking, shift management), Talent (performance, recruitment, engagement, training), Finance (expenses, procurement, project management) and IT (SaaS management, device management, cybersecurity).

Recent product development has focused on embedding artificial intelligence across these hubs, including an AI-assisted applicant tracking system, CV parsing, receipt OCR for expense reports, AI meeting summaries and a natural-language reporting assistant that answers plain-language questions about workforce data.

Because there is no single fixed price list, prospective customers get a tailored quote based on headcount and selected hubs, and a free trial is available so teams can test hub combinations before signing a contract.
